title: CEO Interview with Brice Cruchon, CEO of Dracula Technologies - Semiwiki
gist: Dracula Technologies CEO Brice Cruchon argues indoor organic photovoltaic (OPV) energy harvesting is moving from niche to mainstream power source for battery-free IoT, citing a new generation of its LAYER technology that works down to 5 lux and delivers a 30% performance gain, backed by the world's largest OPV digital-printing factory in Valence, France and partnerships with STMicroelectronics, Schneider Electric, Semtech and others.

insights.claim: Dracula Technologies' latest-generation LAYER organic photovoltaic technology can generate usable energy from indoor light levels as low as 5 lux, targeting applications in extremely low illumination.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: 5-lux operation is the core technical moat for indoor harvesting; watch whether competitors can match this floor, as it defines the addressable set of low-light IoT deployments.
insights.quote: Our latest generation can generate usable energy from light levels as low as 5 lux, allowing us to address applications operating in extremely low indoor illumination.
insights.locator: What does the competitive landscape look like

insights.claim: Dracula's newest OPV generation delivers a 30 percent increase in overall performance while improving operation specifically under indoor LED lighting.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A 30% step-change plus LED optimization widens the range of devices that can run battery-free indoors, expanding the serviceable market with each generation.
insights.quote: It delivers a 30 percent increase in overall performance while improving operation under indoor LED lighting.
insights.locator: What new features and technologies are you working on

insights.claim: LAYER is engineered specifically for low and variable indoor lighting, a distinct problem from outdoor solar because indoor light is far weaker and application-dependent, requiring purpose-built materials and device architectures.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Frames indoor harvesting as a separate technical category from outdoor PV, meaning generic solar players are not direct competitors and specialization is the barrier to entry.
insights.quote: Indoor environments present unique challenges. Light levels are much lower than outdoors and vary depending on the application.
insights.locator: The energy harvesting market includes several technologies

insights.claim: Dracula offers LAYER Vault, an integrated solution combining its OPV harvesting with energy storage to reduce OEM system-design complexity and accelerate time to market for autonomous connected devices.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Bundling storage moves Dracula up the value chain from component to subsystem, raising ASP and stickiness with OEM customers.

insights.claim: Dracula's Green Micropower Factory in Valence, France is described as the world's largest facility dedicated exclusively to producing organic photovoltaic modules using its patented digital printing process.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Owning the largest dedicated OPV production line signals a manufacturing lead that is hard to replicate quickly; capacity utilization is the key metric to track for scale-up execution.
insights.quote: our Green Micropower Factory in Valence, France, is the world's largest facility dedicated exclusively to the production of organic photovoltaic modules using our patented digital printing process.
insights.locator: Over the past decade, we have developed expertise

insights.claim: The company positions moving from lab-innovated materials to repeatable high-volume manufacturing as the single biggest challenge in organic electronics and its greatest competitive strength.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Implies most OPV rivals are stuck at the lab stage; the differentiator to underwrite is manufacturability, not just cell efficiency.
insights.quote: Moving from innovative materials to repeatable, high-volume manufacturing is one of the biggest challenges in organic electronics.
insights.locator: Many organizations develop innovative materials in the laboratory

insights.claim: Dracula argues that raw performance is insufficient to win because customers demand technologies they can manufacture, integrate and deploy reliably at scale.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive battleground from efficiency benchmarks to deployability, favoring incumbents with integration and volume capability.
insights.quote: Performance is important, but performance alone isn't enough.

insights.claim: The core thesis is that billions of connected IoT devices cannot continue relying on disposable batteries, shifting indoor energy harvesting from a niche to a mainstream demand as deployments scale and sustainability becomes a priority.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: This is the secular tailwind underpinning the whole business; the investable question is timing of the niche-to-mainstream inflection.
insights.quote: When we founded Dracula Technologies, we were convinced that billions of connected devices could not continue to rely on disposable batteries.

insights.claim: Semiconductor progress in cutting device power consumption is a direct causal enabler of energy harvesting, making harvesting increasingly practical as chips become more efficient.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Ties Dracula's addressable market to the ultra-low-power chip roadmap; each efficiency gain from MCU/radio vendors expands harvestable applications.
insights.quote: As devices become more energy efficient, energy harvesting becomes an increasingly practical way to power them.
insights.locator: The semiconductor industry has made tremendous progress

insights.claim: The real economic pain point is not the sensor but powering and maintaining large deployments over many years, since replacing thousands or millions of batteries becomes a major operational and financial burden.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Positions the value proposition around lifetime maintenance-cost avoidance rather than device cost, the argument OEMs use to justify adoption at scale.
insights.quote: Replacing one battery is easy. Replacing thousands or even millions of batteries quickly becomes a significant operational and financial challenge.
insights.locator: For many of our customers, the challenge is not the sensor

insights.claim: Dracula asserts there is no single killer application for indoor harvesting, with new use cases emerging every year wherever eliminating battery maintenance creates value.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A diversified-demand thesis reduces single-market dependency but also implies a longer, fragmented go-to-market rather than one concentrated volume driver.
insights.quote: One thing we've learned is that there isn't a single killer application.

insights.claim: Smart buildings are Dracula's fastest-growing market, with ambient-light-powered smart thermostats and occupancy/environmental sensors developed alongside partners Schneider Electric and Orioma.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Schneider Electric validation in the top-growth segment is the strongest commercial proof point; track whether these move from co-development to volume orders.
insights.quote: Smart buildings are one of our fastest-growing markets.
insights.locator: Smart buildings are one of our fastest-growing markets

insights.claim: In smart labels, asset tracking and logistics, Dracula has built ambient-light-powered RFID and Bluetooth Low Energy tracking solutions with Linxens, Paragon ID, MOKOSmart and truvami for retail, warehouse and supply-chain use.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Smart labels are a high-volume, low-cost category; success here would be the clearest path to unit-volume scale for OPV.
insights.locator: Another major focus is smart labels

insights.claim: Dracula's CoolR collaboration shows ambient-light harvesting powering connected retail monitoring in refrigerated environments where battery replacement is especially costly.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Cold-chain/refrigerated retail is a high-pain niche where the maintenance-cost argument is strongest, a useful beachhead example.
insights.locator: We are also seeing strong momentum in smart retail

insights.claim: Dracula's Atmosic Technologies collaboration targets battery-free remote controls and other ultra-low-power connected devices designed for long lifetimes with minimal maintenance.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Battery-free remotes are a consumer-scale application; pairing with low-power radio SoC vendor Atmosic points to a reference design push.

insights.claim: Dracula works with technology ecosystem partners STMicroelectronics, e-peas and Semtech, and is collaborating with Powercast to combine RF wireless power with LAYER OPV for battery-free Bluetooth Low Energy sensor nodes.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Hybrid RF-plus-light harvesting with Powercast plus tier-1 chip partners signals ecosystem integration that lowers OEM adoption friction.
insights.locator: we continue to work closely with technology ecosystem partners

insights.claim: Dracula's innovation is protected by a portfolio of 30 patents across nine internationally recognized patent families spanning organic materials, photovoltaic device architecture and manufacturing processes.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A 30-patent / 9-family portfolio quantifies the IP moat protecting both the materials and the printing process; a defensibility metric for due diligence.
insights.quote: our innovation is supported by a portfolio of 30 patents across nine internationally recognized patent families
insights.locator: Innovation is not only about improving efficiency

insights.claim: Dracula frames OPV as only one application of a broader organic-electronics platform and has announced it is exploring applying its materials and printing expertise to OLED technology.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: OLED optionality signals a potential TAM expansion beyond harvesting; an early-stage, watch-only catalyst rather than a committed roadmap.
insights.quote: we recently announced that we are exploring the potential of applying this expertise to OLED technology.
insights.locator: While OPV remains our core business

insights.claim: Dracula Technologies, founded in 2011, has transitioned from a deep-tech startup to an industrial company and is now pursuing international expansion to make light energy harvesting a mainstream power source.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The startup-to-industrial transition plus international expansion marks the commercialization/scaling phase, the stage where execution and capital needs matter most.
insights.locator: Under his leadership, Dracula Technologies has evolved
